hints.amate: 'amate' means 'love!' but does not appear in this phrase

hints.to be diligent: English 'diligent' is related, but 'diligere' means 'to love/esteem'

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd plural would be 'diligitis' — 'Diligite' is imperative
hints.subjunctive: Subjunctive would be 'diligatis' — 'Diligite' is the imperative plural
hints.perfect: Perfect would be 'dilexistis' — 'Diligite' is the imperative

hints.eligo, eligere, elegi, electum: These are principal parts of 'eligo' (to choose)
hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are principal parts of 'lego' (to read/gather)
hints.deleo, delere, delevi, deletum: These are principal parts of 'deleo' (to destroy)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'inimici' — 'inimicos' with -os is accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'inimicis' — 'inimicos' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'inimicorum' — 'inimicos' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'inimici' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'inimici' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -us — 'inimici' ends in -i
